coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (Ⅱ) ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Libo
Yu, Bin
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S/C-dots) with high fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY, 25%) was successfully synthesized by a one-step microwave-assisted method. In comparison with nitrogen doped C-dots (N/C-dots) prepared using the same method, the resulting N,S/C-dots featured small particle size, uniform surface state, insensitive FL properties to excitation wavelengths and environmental conditions, negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Simultaneous doping of N and S effectively promoted electron-transfer and coordination interaction between N,S/C-dots and Hg2+. Thus, when used as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ label-free detection, the resulting N,S/C-dots showed good detection sensitivity and ion selectivity. The limit of detection was 2μM; among 15 metal ions investigated, only Fe3+ showed interference to the Hg2+ detection. Fortunately, this interference could be effectively shielded using a chelating agent sodium hexametaphoshpate. The applicability of N,S/C-dots as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection in lake water and tap water was demonstrated. Finally, based on its favorable features of negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the N,S/C-dots were successfully applied to probe Hg2+ in living cells, which broaden its application in biological system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hg2+ Ions detection
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism for improved FL properties of N,S/C-dots
Bio-imaging of Hg2+ in living cells
Possible FL quenching mechanism of Hg2+ to N,S/C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21222505 and 21475124 ) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2014T70301 ). The authors have declared no conflict of interest. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution of N,S/C-dot, (B) XRD, (C) Raman and (D) XPS spectra of N/C-dots and N,S/C-dots.

(A) UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of N,S/C-dots. Inset: N,S/C-dots solution under white (left) and 365nm UV (right) light illumination. (B) FL spectra of N,S/C-dots excited at different wavelengths in the range of 305–355nm. Inset: FL spectra when excited at wavelengths above 365nm. (C) FL decay spectrum of N,S/C-dots. (D) Effect of irradiation time at 353nm, (E) ionic strength, and (F) pH values on FL intensity of N,S/C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Plot of quenched FL intensity (ΔF) of 0.015mg/mL N,S/C-dots against Hg2+ concentration. The inset shows the linear dependence of ΔF on Hg2+ concentration. (B) Effect of EDTA on the FL properties of N,S/C-dots and 25μM Hg2+-quenched N,S/C-dots. (C) Effect of different metal ions (50μM) on the FL properties of N,S/C-dots. (D) FL spectra of N,S/C-dots with/without the presence of 25μM Hg2+ as the quenching agent, 1mM Fe3+ as interfering agent, and 1mM SHPP as chelating agent to Fe3+.

(A) Cell viability of QSG7701 cells in the presence of different concentrations of N/C-dots and N,S/C-dots. FL microscopy images of (B) control Changliver cells, (C) incubated with N,S/C-dots for 5h at 37°C and (D) further incubated with Hg2+ for another 1h at 37°C.
The proposed FL quenching mechanism for N,S/C-dots/Hg2+ system.
Performance comparison of the proposed sensor with other methods for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (Ⅱ) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Jilin University, Changchun, Jilin 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S/C-dots) with high fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY, 25%) was successfully synthesized by a one-step microwave-assisted method. In comparison with nitrogen doped C-dots (N/C-dots) prepared using the same method, the resulting N,S/C-dots featured small particle size, uniform surface state, insensitive FL properties to excitation wavelengths and environmental conditions, negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Simultaneous doping of N and S effectively promoted electron-transfer and coordination interaction between N,S/C-dots and Hg2+. Thus, when used as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ label-free detection, the resulting N,S/C-dots showed good detection sensitivity and ion selectivity. The limit of detection was 2μM; among 15 metal ions investigated, only Fe3+ showed interference to the Hg2+ detection. Fortunately, this interference could be effectively shielded using a chelating agent sodium hexametaphoshpate. The applicability of N,S/C-dots as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection in lake water and tap water was demonstrated. Finally, based on its favorable features of negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the N,S/C-dots were successfully applied to probe Hg2+ in living cells, which broaden its application in biological system.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have recently been actively studied as a new member of carbonaceous fluorescent (FL) material owing to their unique optical properties, resistance to photo-bleaching, water solubility, low toxicity, and biocompatibility (Dong et al., 2013; Li, H., et al., 2012; Bao et al., 2015). As results, C-dots has found wide potential applications, including bioimaging or biosensors (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2010; Kong et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012; Liu, X., et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2014; Liu, Y., et al., 2014; Wang and Zhou, 2014), drug delivery (Tang et al., 2013; Karthik et al., 2013; Choi et al., 2014; Zheng et al., 2014), electrochemical/electrochemiluminescence catalysis/sensing (Liu and Chen, 2013; Yang et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2013; Long et al., 2014), energy conversion (Li et al., 2010; Tang et al., 2012; Zhang, H., et al., 2012), photocatalysis, and optoelectronics (Li, H., et al., 2012). In the field of FL sensor applications, however, critical challenges remain for C-dots, particularly in terms of the low fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY), as well as relatively poor probe sensitivity and selectivity.

In order to improve FL properties of C-dots, doping with semiconductors and surface modification or passivation have been explored (Ding et al., 2014). For example, C-dots doped with ZnS and functionalized with oligomeric poly(ethylenelycol) diamine (PEG 1500N) have shown very high FLQY up to 78% (Anilkumar et al., 2011). Heteroatom (e.g., N, B, S, and Si) doping with or without surface modifications (Qian et al., 2014; Chandra et al., 2013; Hu, Y., et al., 2014), especially N-doping or co-doping with N and another element, has also been actively investigated to improve the FLQY of C-dots (Liu et al., 2012; Zhang, Y.Q., et al. 2012; Gao et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2014; Wei et al., 2014; Qu et al., 2014). By using citric acid and urea as C and N sources, respectively, Qu et al. (2012) reported a one-step microwave synthesis of N-doped C-dots (N/C-dots) with FLQY of about 14%. Jahan et al. (2013) reported the preparation of surface passivated, N and B co-doped C-dots with high FLQY (23.71%). The C-dots prepared could be used to detect photosensitizer and carcinogenic dyes with good sensitivity and selectivity. N and S co-doped C-dots (N,S/C-dots) were also prepared without using passivation reagents from precursors of l-cysteine and citric acid, or from hair fiber, gentamycin sulfate and rice (Sun et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2014; Hu, Q., et al., 2014). The FLQY of N,S/C-dots has been found to be 73% (Dong et al., 2013). Nevertheless, an efficient method for preparation of C-dots with high FLQY is yet to be developed (Baker and Baker, 2010; Shen et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011), and C-dots having high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for sensor applications are still rarely known.

On the other hand, Hg2+ represents a serious threat to the environment and human health due to its high toxicity and easy accumulation in human body (Bera et al., 2014). There has been an increasing interest to develop highly efficient methods for Hg2+ detection. Among the current Hg2+ detection methods including atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy, spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and sensor systems (Nolan and Lippard, 2008; Aragay et al., 2011), fluorophore-based sensors have received great attention due to their high sensitivity, good selectivity, and fast analysis (Yang et al., 2005; Ko et al., 2006; Wang, C., et al. 2014; Shi et al., 2011). However, wide application of fluorophore-based sensors in Hg2+ detection requires water-compatible fluorescent probe which can work effectively in aqueous solution (Bera et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2014; Zhang and Zhu, 2014). In addressing this, C-dots-based fluorescent nanoprobes having good water solubility has recently been actively explored for Hg2+ or methylmercury detection (Costas-Mora et al., 2014; Zhang and Chen, 2014; Wang, B., et al., 2014; Cui et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2013). However, readily available C-dots with high sensitivity and selectivity to Hg2+ and suitable for biological application is still not known.

Herein, we report a new N and S co-doped C-dots (N,S/C-dots) prepared by an easy one-step microwave-assisted method (See Supporting information). The as-obtained N,S/C-dots possessed a graphitic-like structure with narrow size distribution and uniform surface state. The fluorescent property of N,S/C-dots was found independent to the excitation wavelength and insensitive to environmental conditions. When used as FL probe for Hg2+ label-free detection, the resulting N,S/C-dots showed good detection sensitivity and selectivity. For further widen its application in biological system, intracellular Hg2+ imaging was successfully demonstrated.

For the preparation of N,S/C-dots with desired high FL properties, effect of l-cysteine content was first evaluated. It was found that, at a mass ratio of citric acid/urea/l-cysteine of 16/16/4 and with a reaction time of 5min, strong UV absorption and FL emission could be obtained (Fig. S-1). FLQY further confirmed the optimal conditions (Table S-1). The N,S/C-dots showed excellent water solubility. No obvious agglomeration or precipitation was observed within a six-month period at room temperature for 10mg/mL N,S/C-dots aqueous solution.

TEM picture shows uniformly dispersed N,S/C-dots with average particle size of 1.1±0.2nm ( Fig. 1A). The size distribution of the N,S/C-dots is narrow with the range of 0.8–1.8nm (inset of Fig. 1A). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra reveal two diffraction peaks centered at around 2θ=14.9° and 25.5° for both of N/C-dots and N,S/C-dots (Fig. 1B), which is similar to those previously reported N/C-dots or heteroatom-free C-dots (Du et al., 2014), and confirms the graphene-like structure of N,S/C-dots. Raman spectra (Fig. 1C) also show two characteristic peaks for both N/C-dots and N,S/C-dots at 1365cm−1 and 1585cm−1, attributable to the disordered D band and the crystalline G band, respectively. The intensity ratio of D band to G band (I D/I G) was found to be 0.76 for N,S/C-dots, smaller than that of N/C-dots (0.87), suggesting reduced content of sp3 carbon, higher graphitization degree, and more uniform surface state of N,S/C-dots than N/C-dots (Hu, Y., et al., 2014; Li, Y., et al., 2012).

Chemical compositions of N/C-dots and N,S/C-dots were analyzed by XPS and FT-IR. The successful incorporation of S atoms into the carbon-rich C-dots was confirmed by the XPS analysis (Fig. 1D). For N/C-dots, wide-scan XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 285.0, 400.0, 532.0eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively. For N,S/C-dots, a new peak at 163.0eV corresponding to S2p is observed (inset of Fig. 1D). The core-level C1s spectrum of N,S/C-dots can be deconvoluted into six peaks at 284.3eV (sp2 C), 284.8eV (sp3 C), 285.3eV (C–S), 285.7eV (C–N), 287.4eV (C–O), and 288.4eV (C=N/C=O), respectively (Fig. S-2A) (Sun et al., 2013). In comparison, N,S/C-dots has a higher sp2 C/sp3 C ratio of 4.80 than N/C-dots (2.96) (Fig. S-2D), indicating an increased sp2 C content. This agrees well with previous Raman results. For N,S/C-dots, the high-resolution N1s spectrum shows the presence of pyridinic N (399.3eV), pyrrolic N (400.1eV), and graphitic N (401.2eV) (Fig. S-2B), which is similar to that for N/C-dots (Fig. S-2E). S atoms in N,S/C-dots takes the form of C–S–C, as evidenced by the core-level spectrum of S2p (Fig. S-2C), which can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 163.0 and 164.0eV, corresponding to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of thiophene S (Dong et al., 2013).

FT-IR spectra of N/C-dots and N,S/C-dots are comparable and show no characteristic absorptions of precursors (i.e., citric acid, urea, and l-cysteine) (Fig. S-3). The broad peaks at 3050–3552cm−1 can be assigned to υN–H and υO–H. The presence of such functional groups contributes to the excellent water solubility of the prepared C-dots (Qu et al., 2012, 2014). The absorption peaks at 1638cm−1 and 1740cm−1 indicate the presence of carbonyl functional groups in the C-dots. The formation of C–N and/or C–S bonds is evidenced by absorption peaks at ca. 1195cm−1.

0.1mg/mL N,S/C-dots aqueous solution shows three UV absorption peaks at 242, 270, and 340nm ( Fig. 2A). The two absorptions at 242 and 270nm, attributable to the π–π⁎ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and the n–π⁎ transition of C=O bond, respectively, lead to no observable fluorescence (Hu, Q., et al., 2014). Whereas the broad adsorption centered at ca. 340nm, resulted from the trapping of excited carriers by the localized surface states (Dong et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2012, 2014), leads to strong FL emission. In comparison, aqueous N/C-dots solution (0.1mg/mL) shows an additional strong absorption peak at ca. 404nm, whose magnitude decreases with increasing S content in N,S/C-dots, and completely diminished at a mass ratio of 16/16/4 for citric acid/urea/l-cysteine (Fig. S-1C). This suggests that the surface state band structure of N,S/C-dots is more uniform and has less defects than that of N/C-dots.

N,S/C-dots and N/C-dots exhibit similar yellow and bright blue when excited with white and UV (365nm) light, respectively (inset of Fig. 2A and Fig. S-4A). However, N,S/C-dots showed the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 353 and 450nm (Fig. 2A), which are 49 and 75nm blue-shifted in relative to those of N/C-dots (Fig. S-4A). Moreover, different from most C-dots and N/C-dots, the emission of N,S/C-dots is nearly excitation-independent in the wavelength range of 305–365nm (Figs. 2B and S-4B). The full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the FL emission peak for N,S/C-dots (88nm) is much narrower than that for N/C-dots (110nm). All of these can be attributed to a more uniform localized surface state band structure and narrower size distribution of N,S/C-dots than that of N/C-dots (Bao et al., 2015; Liu, Y., et al., 2014; Hu, Y., et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2014).

By fitting FL decay curve with a double-exponential function, a FL lifetime of 9.83ns was calculated for the N,S/C-dots excited at 377nm (Fig. 2C), longer than that of N/C-dots (6.97ns). Using quinine sulfate as standard, the FLQY of N,S/C-dots was calculated to be 25.2%, which is 1.8-fold higher than that of N/C-dots.

To understand the environmental stability, N,S/C-dots solution (0.015mg/mL) was first irradiated continuously with a beam of UV light at 353nm and the FL intensity was found to be changed negligibly in the period time of 3h, demonstrating a good photostability (Fig. 2D). Moreover, the emissive property of N,S/C-dots is insensitive to ionic strength and pH values. The FL intensity remained unchanged at high ionic strength up to 1mol/L or in the pH range of 5.0–10.0 (Fig. 2E and F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for improved FL properties of N,S/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the defects mechanism and isolated sp2 conjugated carbon structures mechanism are favorable to FL enhancement of C-dots by heteroatom (Qian et al., 2014). However, in the present work, Raman, XPS results and the excitation-independent emission properties of N,S/C-dots have revealed that surface of N,S/C-dots are more uniform than that of N/C-dots. Therefore, we deduce that isolated sp2 conjugated carbon clusters contribute to the improved FL performance of co-doped C-dots. As illustrated in UV–vis measurement, the π–π conjugated structure of the prepared C-dots decrease with the increase of S content, but XPS and Raman results show that the C sp2/sp3 ratio increases with the doping of S atoms. The phenomena can demonstrate that more isolated sp2 conjugated carbon clusters, which can be used as fluorophores, are formed with the incorporation of S atoms. The possible structure of the N,S/C-dots based on the above analysis is present in Scheme 1A.

For detection of Hg2+, different amounts of mercury nitrate solution were added into N,S/C-dots blank solution. The resulting solutions were incubated for 5min, and then the FL emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 353nm. In this study, the applicability of the resulting N,S/C-dots as FL probe for label-free detection of Hg2+ was first demonstrated by adding 50μM Hg2+ to a 0.015mg/mL aq. N,S/C-dots solution and monitoring the FL emission at 445nm. Significant FL quenching, accompanied by blue shift of emission wavelength to 429nm, was observed upon addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S-5A). Such a FL quenching effect of Hg2+ on N,S/C-dots can be attributed to electron transfer from excited N,S/C-dots to Hg2+ (Costas-Mora et al., 2014). In order to determine the detection sensitivity to Hg2+, FL property of aq. N,S/C-dots solutions (0.015mg/mL) containing varying amounts of Hg2+ was investigated (Fig. S-5B). It was found that, with increase of Hg2+ concentration, the FL intensity of N,S/C-dots decreased gradually. A nearly complete quenching of FL emission occurred at a Hg2+ concentration of ca. 300μM. Fig. 3A plots the quenched FL intensity ΔF (ΔF=F 0−F, where F 0 and F represent the FL intensity of N,S/C-dots without and with the presence of Hg2+, respectively) against the concentration of Hg2+. A linear dependence of ΔF on the concentration of Hg2+ up to 40μM was found (inset of Fig. 3A), with a linear regression equation of ΔF=16.02C−13.93 and correlation coefficient of 0.994 (n=3), where C is the concentration of Hg2+. The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ using the developed method was determinated to be 2μM. Compared with non-C-dots-based sensors ( Table 1), although LOD of our method is not very satisfactory, it has outstanding advantages including wider linear range, simple preparation procedure and allowable application in aqueous solution. In comparison with other similar C-dots-based method, FLQY of N,S/C-dots prepared in the present work is calculated to be highest, which thus widen its application scope. Moreover, the method is simple, fast and N,S/C-dots prepared herein are remarkably stable under extreme environmental conditions including ionic strength, pH value and UV light illumination.

The reversibility of the proposed N,S/C-dots probe was evaluated by using EDTA as a signal recovering agent. As shown in Fig. 3B, when 1mM EDTA was added to 0.015mg/mL N,S/C-dots blank solution, about 1% of quenching of FL signal (i.e., ΔF/F 0=1%) was found; 45% of quenching resulted upon addition of 25μM Hg2+. However, when EDTA was added to Hg2+ quenched aq. N,S/C-dots solution, the FL emission recovered to 97% of its initial intensity. Therefore, a “turn-off” and “turn-on” sensor can be potentially fabricated.

Ion selectivity of N,S/C-dots FL probe was evaluated by adding 50μM different metal ions into 0.015mg/mL aq. N,S/C-dots solution and measuring the FL intensity. It can be seen from Fig. 3C that, among the 15 different metal ions, most of them have no or little quenching effect on the FL property of N,S/C-dots, indicating an excellent ion selectivity. However, one metal ion, i.e., Fe3+, was found capable of quenching N,S/C-dots to its 78% of FL intensity. Although the quenching efficiency of Fe3+ is only ca. 1/3 of that of Hg2+, it is expected that Fe3+ would interfere with the Hg2+ detection. In addressing this problem, a chelating agent SHPP (sodium hexametaphoshpate) was used to effectively shield the interference from Fe3+ (Liu et al., 2012). As shown in Fig. 3D, the addition of SHPP to aq. N,S/C-dots blank solution showed no discernible effect on the FL property (Line b). In the same figure, it shows that a) 43% FL intensity was quenched as the result of addition of 25μM Hg2+ (Line c); b) further 33% FL quenching resulted by introducing additional 1mM Fe3+ (Line d). This quenched FL emission, however, can be recovered to its 80% of value by adding 1mM SHPP to the quenched solution (Line e). It is noted that the recovered FL intensity (i.e., F=0.8×F 0) is higher than the FL intensity of aq. N,S/C-dots solution quenched solely by Hg2+ (i.e., F=0.57×F 0). This may be attributed to the interaction between Hg2+ and SHPP (Line f).

The applicability of the proposed methods in real sample analysis was evaluated using a standard addition method for detection of Hg2+ in lake water and tap water. Both samples were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 15min and filtered through a 0.22μm filtering membrane prior to the experiment. After adding 10, 15 and 20μM Hg2+ into lake and tap water, satisfactory recoveries between 95.4% and 110% were obtained (Table S-2). The assay shows good accuracy and reproducibility for Hg2+ detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging of Hg2+ in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to extend application of N,S/C-dots in biological system, the cytotoxicity of N,S/C-dots was first evaluated through the MTT assays using QSG7701 cells as target ( Fig. 4A). After incubated with different concentration of N,S/C-dots even up to 1000μg/mL for 48h, no visible reduction in the viability of QSG7701 cells is obtained. While for N/C-dots, the cell viability is much lower than that of N,S/C-dots, indicating low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility of N,S/C-dots, which are favorable for monitoring Hg2+ in living cells.

FL bio-imaging of intracellular Hg2+ were conducted subsequently to confirm the availability of the as-prepared N,S/C-dots (Treatment procedure see Supporting information). It can be seen from Fig. 4B that no fluorescence could be observed in control Changliver cells (without incubation with N,S/C-dots). As expected, after incubated with 0.4mg/mL N,S/C-dots at 37°C for 5h, the intracellular region of Changliver cells emitted green FL upon UV illumination (Fig. 4C). However, the FL intensity were significantly weaken upon the addition of exogenous Hg2+ into the N,S/C-dots-pretreated Changliver cells system (Fig. 4D), which should be attributed to the quench effect of Hg2+ on N,S/C-dots. This therefore indicate that the as-synthesized N,S/C-dots possess great potential in serving as optical nanoprobes for intracellular Hg2+ quantitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible FL quenching mechanism of Hg2+ to N,S/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that FL emission of most C-dots derives from the radiative recombination of excitons, which can be quenched by metal ions such as Hg2+ or Fe3+ via nonradiative electron-transfer between C-dots and metal ions (Zhu et al., 2013). However, it might be a little different for N,S/C-dots. Scheme 1 depicts the physical properties of N,S/C-dots (Fig. S-6 and Table S-3) and possible quenching mechanism of N,S/C-dots/Hg2+ system. As shown in Scheme 1A, it exist some oxygen-containing groups on the surface of N,S/C-dots, which can coordinate with Hg2+ to form complexes (Liu et al., 2013), leading to the transfer of electron from N,S/C-dots to Hg2+ (Scheme 1B). Interestingly, this electron transfer can be facilitated by the doping of S atoms. According to the previous report (Li et al., 2014), although N (electronegativity: 3.05) and S (electronegativity: 2.58) atoms are both electron donor, S atoms own smaller electronegativity and larger atomic radius, leading to the valence electrons of S in the third shell tend to lose easily. Consequently, introduction of S into the graphitic-like N,S/C-dots should effectively tune the electronic local density of N,S/C-dots, which would promote the coordination interaction between oxygen functional groups on the edge of N,S/C-dots and Hg2+. The fast electron transfer process and promoted interaction, therefore, causes significant FL quenching of N,S/C-dots/Hg2+ system (Scheme 1B right).

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S/C-dots) with small size of 1.1nm, high FLQY of 25%, remarkable stability under environmental conditions, negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility were successfully prepared through a facile one-step microwave-assisted method. Co-doping of N and S atoms effectively promoted electron-transfer rate and coordination interaction between N,S/C-dots and Hg2+. Thereby, when used as a FL probe for label-free Hg2+ detection, the resultant N,S/C-dots was found to have high sensitivity and ion selectivity. The applicability of N,S/C-dots as a “turn-off” FL probe for label-free Hg2+ detection in real water sample and living cell were demonstrated, which broaden its application in serving as potential nanoprobes for bio-imaging in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21222505 and 21475124) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014T70301).
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.06.050.
